Choose the single best answer for each item. Your responses are recorded when you submit. Scores, correct answers, and explanations are not shown after submission — your results will be reviewed and distributed to you by your program.

Q1
A 45-year-old man presents with dyspnea and petechiae. He is pale and tired. Labs: WBC 12, Hb 7, Plt 35, INR 1.2, PTT 24. Liver and kidney function are normal. LDH 1000. Smear shows abundant schistocytes, with high reticulocytes, high LDH, low haptoglobin, and a NEGATIVE DAT. ADAMTS13 activity returns at 45%.



Which interpretation best fits this picture?
  • AEvans syndrome — the negative DAT excludes immune hemolysis
  • BDIC — the INR and PTT confirm consumptive coagulopathy
  • CAcquired (autoimmune) TTP — begin plasma exchange and corticosteroids
  • DThe ADAMTS13 level argues against TTP; a complement-mediated TMA (aHUS) or another secondary TMA should be pursued
  • ECongenital TTP — confirm with ADAMTS13 gene analysis
Correct answer: D — ADAMTS13 >10% points away from TTP and toward aHUS or another secondary TMA.

an ADAMTS13 activity >10% should make you “think of aHUS,” not TTP. Severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (typically <10%) defines TTP; with autoantibodies it is acquired, without them it is congenital. At 45% activity, TTP is not supported despite the classic MAHA + thrombocytopenia, so the reasoning must shift to complement-mediated or secondary TMA. Normal coagulation (INR 1.2, PTT 24) argues against DIC, and the MAHA picture with schistocytes is not Evans syndrome.

Q2
A 34-year-old woman is admitted with immune-mediated thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (iTTP). She is receiving plasma exchange, corticosteroids, and rituximab. On the second hospital day, she asks:

"I read online about a new medication called caplacizumab. Would I benefit from it?"

Which of the following is the most accurate response based on the HERCULES trial?
  • AReduce TTP relapse
  • BReduced overall mortality
  • CFaster platelet recovery
  • Ddecrease bleeding but increase thromboembolic complications
Correct answer:

Q3
A 60-year-old woman has anemia, thrombocytopenia (Plt 28), and schistocytes on smear. Before ADAMTS13 results return, the team notes MCV 116, mild pancytopenia, . LDH is markedly elevated but haptoglobin is only borderline low and the reticulocyte count is LOW.



Which is the most important next consideration before committing to plasma exchange?
  • AStart caplacizumab empirically while awaiting ADAMTS13
  • BSevere vitamin B12 deficiency can mimic TTP (pseudo-TTP) — check B12 before assuming TTP
  • CBegin eculizumab for presumed aHUS
  • DThe low reticulocyte count confirms TTP
  • ETransfuse platelets to a target >50 before any further workup
Correct answer: B — Severe B12 deficiency is a recognized TTP mimic (“pseudo-TTP”).

severe B12 deficiency among the mimics of TTP/aHUS. Intramedullary hemolysis from megaloblastic anemia produces high LDH, low haptoglobin and fragmented cells that can resemble MAHA, but the macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils and inappropriately LOW reticulocyte count (true TTP drives a brisk reticulocytosis) are the clues that this is megaloblastic pseudo-TTP. Treating this with PLEX delays correct therapy. Platelet transfusion is generally avoided in true TTP.

Q4
A patient with confirmed acquired TTP is started on daily plasma exchange. The team wants to add caplacizumab.

Which statement about caplacizumab is correct, and what is its principal drawback?
  • AIt is a C5 inhibitor; main risk is meningococcal infection
  • BIt depletes anti-ADAMTS13 antibodies; main risk is hepatitis B reactivation
  • CIt replaces ADAMTS13 enzyme; main risk is anaphylaxis
  • DIt inhibits the GPIIb/IIIa receptor; main risk is thrombocytopenia
  • EIt is a humanized single-domain antibody against the A1 domain of vWF that blocks vWF–platelet interaction
Correct answer: E — Caplacizumab targets the vWF A1 domain; the chief drawback is bleeding.

caplacizumab is a humanized single-domain antibody specifically targeting the A1 domain of vWF, preventing ultra-large vWF multimers from binding platelets and thereby blocking microvascular platelet-rich thrombi. Benefits listed are rapid platelet recovery, faster resolution of neurological symptoms, and decreased relapse. The drawback the lecture identifies is increased bleeding risk, especially mucocutaneous bleeding. It does not deplete antibodies, replace enzyme, or block complement.

Q5
You are deciding on a complement inhibitor for a patient with aHUS and reviewing landmark practice points.

Which statement reflects the evidence as presented?
  • ADGKE-associated aHUS responds especially well to C5 blockade
  • BEculizumab is preferred over ravulizumab because of a shorter half-life
  • CLiver transplantation is contraindicated in all aHUS
  • DPlasma exchange is curative in complement-mediated aHUS
  • ERavulizumab is favored over eculizumab largely because of its longer half-life
Correct answer: E — Ravulizumab's advantages are its longer half-life and cost-effectiveness.

Q6
A 15-year-old boy is referred from ENT after incidental thrombocytopenia found during a hearing-loss workup (planned cochlear implant). He has had easy bruising and epistaxis since childhood. what's is the diagnosis



Why would a diagnosis of ITP be an error here, and what is the underlying defect?
  • AThis is Bernard-Soulier syndrome
  • BThis is Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome
  • CThis is type 2B von Willebrand disease
  • Da MYH9-related disorder
  • EIt is true ITP; start corticosteroids and IVIG
Correct answer: D — Giant platelets + neutrophil Döhle-like inclusions + deafness = MYH9-related disorder.

The combination of lifelong thrombocytopenia, giant platelets, Döhle-like neutrophil inclusions, and sensorineural hearing loss (cochlear implant workup) is the classic MYH9-related disorder, caused by mutation in MYH9 (myosin IIA). Features include deafness, kidney disease, and (e.g. Epstein syndrome) progressive nephropathy. Labeling this ITP would expose the patient to ineffective immunosuppression and splenectomy. Wiskott-Aldrich produces small platelets; this picture shows large platelets.

Q7
A 27-year-old woman has jaundice, splenomegaly, Hb 8.5, brisk reticulocytosis (ARC 315), indirect hyperbilirubinemia, and LDH 450. DAT is positive for IgG and negative for C3d. She is hemodynamically stable. ANA, RF, HIV/HCV serologies and CT are planned.

After establishing warm AIHA, which test adds the LEAST value to the initial evaluation?
  • ACT chest/abdomen
  • BBone marrow aspiration and biopsy
  • CANA and rheumatoid factor
  • DHIV and hepatitis C serology
  • EHaptoglobin
Correct answer: B — Bone marrow biopsy adds the least value in this initial workup.

With a clear hemolytic picture (high retics, high LDH, indirect bilirubin) and an IgG-positive DAT establishing warm AIHA, the initial evaluation focuses on secondary causes — infections (HIV/HCV), autoimmune disease (ANA/RF), and lymphoproliferative disease (CT) — plus confirming hemolysis (haptoglobin). A marrow biopsy is not part of the routine initial warm-AIHA workup in this stable patient with robust marrow response (the high reticulocyte count already shows an appropriate marrow).

Q8
Months later the same patient returns acutely symptomatic: Hb 5, palpitations, dyspnea, dizziness, DAT positive for IgG. Two units of PRBC are ordered but there are NO compatible units due to a warm autoantibody.

What is the most appropriate next step?
  • AWait for rituximab to take effect before transfusing
  • BGive the least-incompatible PRBC unit
  • CWithhold transfusion and give IVIG only
  • DTransfuse only after a fully crossmatch-compatible unit is found
  • EStart plasmapheresis instead of transfusion
Correct answer: B — In life-threatening warm AIHA with no compatible units, give the least-incompatible unit.

Q9
A 50-year-old woman has macrocytic anemia (MCV 108), Hb 8.7, LDH 510,



Which statement about her disorder is accurate?
  • AMost cold agglutinins are IgG against the i antigen
  • BPrimary CAD usually produces a polyclonal cold agglutinin
  • CAntibodies with higher thermal amplitude cause more severe disease
  • DDestruction is predominantly intravascular
  • EThe smear typically shows marked spherocytosis
Correct answer: C — Higher thermal amplitude antibodies cause more severe cold agglutinin disease.

Cold agglutinin disease is mediated by IgM antibodies (usually anti-I), fixing complement so the DAT is C3d-positive/IgG-negative; clearance is largely extravascular (hepatic, complement-mediated). The clinically important determinant is thermal amplitude — antibodies reactive at temperatures closer to body temperature (higher thermal amplitude) cause more severe disease. Primary CAD is typically a monoclonal (clonal) process, not polyclonal, and spherocytosis is characteristic of warm AIHA, not CAD.

Q10
The CAD patient above worsens despite cold avoidance and folic acid: Hb 5.6, LDH 820.

Which therapy is of LEAST value in this acute setting?
  • ASutimlimab
  • BIVIG
  • CPlasmapheresis
  • DCorticosteroids
  • EPRBC transfusion through a blood warmer
Correct answer: D — Corticosteroids are of least value in cold agglutinin disease.

Unlike warm AIHA, CAD responds poorly to corticosteroids, so methylprednisolone is the least useful option here. Useful measures include transfusion through a warmer, plasmapheresis (removes IgM, which is largely intravascular) for acute control, and the complement (C1s) inhibitor sutimlimab. Definitive control targets the clone (rituximab ± bendamustine) or complement; ibrutinib and splenectomy are noted as unlikely to be effective.

Q11
A 32-year-old man presents with chills, myalgias, jaundice, and black urine. Hb 6.5, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, LDH 1204. DAT is positive for C3d and negative for IgG. A Donath-Landsteiner antibody is detected.

Which statement about this disorder is correct?
  • AThe biphasic IgG antibody binds RBCs in the cold and fixes complement, causing intravascular hemolysis on rewarming, and the antibody is directed against the P antigen
  • BThe antibody is IgM directed against the P antigen, causing intravascular hemolysis on rewarming, and the antibody is directed against the P antigen
  • CIt is primarily a disease of the elderly
  • DAcrocyanosis is the hallmark presenting feature
  • EExtravascular hemolysis is the dominant mechanism
Correct answer: A — Paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria: biphasic Donath-Landsteiner IgG anti-P, intravascular hemolysis.

PCH is caused by the biphasic Donath-Landsteiner antibody — an IgG (not IgM) autoantibody against the P antigen that binds RBCs at cold temperatures and triggers complement-mediated INTRAvascular hemolysis upon rewarming, producing hemoglobinuria (black urine). It is classically a disorder of children, often post-viral, and acrocyanosis is a feature of CAD rather than PCH.

Q12
A patient with a chronic DAT-negative hemolytic anemia is found to have pyruvate kinase deficiency and remains symptomatically anemic.

Which therapy is appropriate for symptomatic anemia in PK deficiency?
  • ALuspatercept
  • BMitapivat
  • CSotatercept
  • DIVIG
  • ERuxolitinib
Correct answer: B — Mitapivat, a pyruvate kinase activator.

Mitapivat is an oral PK activator approved for pyruvate kinase deficiency and is the source-endorsed therapy for symptomatic anemia in this disorder. Luspatercept/sotatercept act on the TGF-β pathway for ineffective erythropoiesis (e.g., thalassemia, MDS), and ruxolitinib (JAK inhibitor) and IVIG have no role here.

Q13
A patient has had two anaphylactic reactions during red-cell transfusion (urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension), with no fever, that were not prevented by antihistamine and steroid premedication. Work-up shows the patient is IgA-deficient with a documented anti-IgA antibody, and no IgA-deficient donor unit is available.

What is the most appropriate way to provide the next red-cell transfusion?
  • AWashed red cells
  • BIrradiated units
  • CPathogen-reduced units
  • DStandard leukoreduced units with higher-dose antihistamine premedication
  • EFresh frozen plasma added to dilute residual antibody
Correct answer: A — Washed red cells (extra washing renders the product IgA-depleted).

Anaphylactic transfusion reactions in an IgA-deficient recipient with anti-IgA are classically driven by donor plasma IgA. When an IgA-deficient donor unit is unavailable, washing the red cells removes residual plasma proteins/antibodies; the source notes that thorough washing makes the product effectively IgA-deficient (<0.05 mg/dL). Washing is indicated for IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA, prior anaphylactic reactions of unknown etiology, and recurrent severe febrile/allergic reactions not controlled by premedication. Leukoreduction and irradiation do not remove plasma IgA, and pathogen reduction does not address the plasma-protein trigger.

Q14
A 64-year-old started on dapsone 100 mg/day three weeks ago presents with dyspnea, cyanosis, and SpO2 74% that does NOT improve with oxygen. ABG: pH 7.70, pCO2 17, PaO2 328, SaO2 (calculated) 97.8%. Hb 7.2, LDH 1120, DAT negative.

What is the diagnosis and the specific treatment if the level is significantly elevated?
  • ADAT-negative warm AIHA; corticosteroids
  • BCarbon monoxide poisoning; hyperbaric oxygen
  • CMethemoglobinemia; methylene blue
  • DHypertensive TMA; plasmapheresis
  • EMegaloblastic anemia; vitamin B12
Correct answer: C — Dapsone-induced methemoglobinemia; treat with methylene blue.

The hallmark is a saturation gap: profound cyanosis and low pulse-oximetry SpO2 that do not correct with oxygen, while the arterial PaO2 is high and the calculated SaO2 from PaO2 is near-normal. Dapsone is a classic oxidant cause of methemoglobinemia. With a methemoglobin level of 25%, the specific antidote is methylene blue (note: avoid methylene blue in G6PD deficiency). This is not warm AIHA, CO poisoning, or a TMA.

Q15
A 29-year-old man from Bangladesh, admitted with pneumonia, hg of 9


All of these can cause this sings in the blood smear except
  • ACombined deficiency of iron and B12/folate only
  • BPyruvate kinase deficiency
  • CRecent red-cell transfusion
  • DThalassemia intermedia from compound heterozygosity for β-thalassemia and hemoglobin E
  • EPure sideroblastic anemia from lead poisoning
Correct answer: Pyruvate kinase deficiency

A dimorphic (dual) red-cell population can be caused by transfusion, sideroblastic anemia, or combined iron and B12/folate deficiency —

Q16
A 49-year-old man, 3 months after kidney transplant, develops progressive normocytic anemia with and normal WBC/platelets.



What is the diagnosis?
  • AAcute erythroid leukemia
  • BMyelodysplastic syndrome with ring sideroblasts
  • CAplastic anemia
  • DMegaloblastic anemia from B12 deficiency
  • EPure red cell aplasia due to parvovirus B19
Correct answer: E — Parvovirus B19 pure red cell aplasia (giant proerythroblasts with intranuclear inclusions).

Isolated normocytic anemia with reticulocytopenia and preserved white cells/platelets in an immunosuppressed transplant patient points to pure red cell aplasia. The pathognomonic marrow finding is giant proerythroblasts containing a single large intranuclear inclusion with a surrounding halo — viral replication of parvovirus B19 in erythroid precursors. The selective erythroid arrest distinguishes it from aplastic anemia (trilineage) and from MDS or erythroleukemia.

Q17
A 24-year-old woman with sickle cell disease continues to experience recurrent vaso-occlusive crises despite hydroxyurea. During her clinic visit, she asks:

"I heard about a new medication called crizanlizumab. Would I benefit from it?"

You explain that the recommendation is based on the SUSTAIN trial. Which of the following best summarizes the study findings?
  • ACrizanlizumab significantly increased hemoglobin concentration without affecting pain crises.
  • BCrizanlizumab eliminated the need for blood transfusions.
  • CCrizanlizumab primarily reduced iron overload.
  • DA & B
  • EAll are incorrect
Correct answer:

Q18
A 55-year-old woman has Hb 10.5, WBC 4, MCV 110, platelets 255. The marrow aspirate



Which diagnosis is most strongly supported, and what cytogenetic rule applies?
  • AMegaloblastic anemia; no cytogenetic criteria
  • BAplastic anemia; normal karyotype required
  • CCML; t(9;22) required
  • DMDS with isolated del(5q); del(5q)
  • EMDS with ring sidroblast, The SF3B1 Gene Mutation
Correct answer: D — MDS with isolated del(5q); del(5q) must be the only abnormality (with limited exceptions).

Macrocytic anemia with hypolobulated/mononuclear megakaryocytes and relatively preserved myeloid maturation is characteristic of MDS with isolated del(5q). , to qualify the del(5q) must be the sole cytogenetic abnormality, although one additional abnormality is permitted as long as it is not a high-risk lesion such as a complex karyotype, monosomy 7, or biallelic TP53. Ring sideroblasts are typically not prominent.

Q19
The landmark Stroke Prevention Trial in Sickle Cell Anemia (STOP) evaluated prophylactic transfusion in children with abnormal transcranial Doppler (TCD) velocities.

what is the result ?
  • APenicillin prophylaxis prevented stroke
  • BHydroxyurea was superior to transfusion for primary stroke prevention regardless of HbF
  • CTransfusion offered no benefit, so TCD screening was abandoned
  • DProphylactic transfusion in children with abnormal TCD reduced stroke
  • EExchange transfusion increased stroke risk
Correct answer: D — STOP showed ~92% stroke reduction with prophylactic transfusion for abnormal TCD.

The STOP-1 trial demonstrated a clear benefit of prophylactic blood transfusion in children with abnormal TCD, a 92% reduction in stroke risk versus observation. This established TCD-based screening and transfusion prophylaxis for high-risk children. Hydroxyurea can be an accepted alternative to chronic transfusion only in specific scenarios (e.g., after ≥1 year of transfusion, with sufficiently high HbF), not as the across-the-board primary-prevention winner.

Q20
A 30-year-old with transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia presents with symptomatic heart failure. Cardiac T2* MRI one week ago is 8 ms.

What is the most appropriate first-line daily chelation regimen?
  • ADeferiprone 100 mg/kg + deferoxamine 50 mg/kg IV over 24 hours
  • BDeferasirox 40 mg/kg + deferoxamine 50 mg/kg over 24 hours
  • CDeferasirox 40 mg/kg + deferiprone 100 mg/kg
  • DDeferasirox 20 mg/kg + deferiprone 70 mg/kg
Correct answer: A — Combined deferiprone + continuous IV deferoxamine for severe cardiac iron loading.

A T2* of 8 ms with overt cardiac failure represents severe cardiac iron overload (T2* <20 ms confers risk; <6 ms carries ~47% risk of CHF within a year). The regimen for symptomatic cardiac siderosis is intensive combination chelation with deferiprone plus continuous 24-hour IV deferoxamine, which is favored for cardioprotection and rapid myocardial iron removal.

Q21
A 30-year-old woman with newly diagnosed AML is admitted for induction chemotherapy and will need red cells and platelets. Her blood group is B-positive; antibody screen is negative. A house officer asks which product modification is most important to prevent a frequently fatal immune complication of cellular transfusion in this setting.

Which modification, and what complication does it prevent?
  • APathogen reduction — to prevent TA-GVHD
  • BIrradiation — to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD)
  • CLeukoreduction — to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease
  • DIrradiation — to prevent transmission of CMV
  • EWashing — to prevent febrile non-hemolytic reactions
Correct answer: B — Irradiation of cellular components prevents TA-GVHD.

Transfusion-associated GVHD occurs when viable donor T-lymphocytes engraft and attack a host who cannot reject them; it presents ~1–2 weeks later with fever, rash, liver dysfunction, diarrhea and then pancytopenia, with ~90% mortality. Gamma/X-ray IRRADIATION of cellular components prevents it and is required for at-risk patients (e.g., HLA-matched or first/second-degree-relative donations, purine analogues, alemtuzumab/ATG, allo/auto SCT, congenital T-cell immunodeficiency, neonates after IUT). Leukoreduction prevents CMV transmission, HLA-alloimmunization and FNHTR but does NOT prevent TA-GVHD; pathogen reduction is an alternative for platelets but irradiation is the established preventive here.

Q22
A 78-year-old man taking apixaban for atrial fibrillation presents with an acute intracerebral hemorrhage. His family asks whether andexanet alfa offers any benefit over standard reversal therapy.

Based on the ANNEXA-I trial, which statement is most accurate?
  • AAndexanet reduced mortality compared to the standard of care
  • BAndexanet improved funcational status at 30 days compared to the standard of care
  • CAndexanet improved funcational status at 30 days & mortality compared to the standard of care
  • DAndexanet Reduced anti-factor Xa activity more compared to standard of care
Correct answer:

Q23
A 14-year-old with NON-transfusion-dependent thalassemia (NTDT) has an LIC of 6 mg Fe/g dry weight; serum ferritin is only mildly elevated.

Which statement correctly guides management?
  • ALIC and ferritin correlate tightly in NTDT
  • BFerritin reliably reflects iron burden in NTDT, so chelation can be guided by ferritin alone
  • CThe mechanism of iron loading is identical to transfusional overload
  • Dstart chelation at LIC ≥5 mg/g, at half the usual TDT starting dose
  • EChelation is never indicated in NTDT because patients are not transfused
Correct answer: D — NTDT iron overload is from enhanced GI absorption; chelate at LIC ≥5 mg/g at half the TDT dose.

In NTDT the predominant mechanism of iron accumulation is increased gastrointestinal absorption driven by inappropriately low hepcidin (from ineffective erythropoiesis), not repeated transfusion. Because ferritin correlates poorly with true iron burden in NTDT, LIC is used: start chelation in patients ≥10 years with LIC ≥5 mg Fe/g dry weight, beginning at half the usual TDT starting dose.

Q24
A transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia program is reviewing luspatercept based on the BELIEVE trial.

Which statement best reflects the evidence as presented?
  • AIt improves mortality compared with placebo
  • BLuspatercept is an erythropoietin analog that stimulates early erythroid progenitors
  • CIt eliminates the need for iron chelation compared with placebo
  • DIt cures the underlying globin defect
  • ELuspatercept binds select TGF-β superfamily ligands to reduce aberrant Smad2/3 signaling
Correct answer: E — Luspatercept acts on TGF-β/Smad2/3 to improve late erythropoiesis and cut transfusion burden.

Luspatercept binds select TGF-β superfamily ligands, reducing aberrant Smad2/3 signaling and promoting late-stage erythroid maturation. In the BELIEVE phase 3 trial a significantly greater proportion of luspatercept patients achieved ≥33% (and ≥50%) reductions in RBC transfusion burden versus placebo. It is not an EPO analog, is not curative, and does not remove the need for chelation.

Q25
A 12-year-old boy presents with pancytopenia, underdeveloped (hypoplastic) thumbs, and patchy cutaneous hyperpigmentation (café-au-lait).

Which statement is correct?
  • ASolid-tumor risk is not increased in this disorder
  • BConditioning must be radiation-free
  • CImmunosuppression is the definitive cure
  • DThe diagnosis is dyskeratosis congenita confirmed by DEB testing
Correct answer: Conditioning must be radiation-free

Radial-ray/thumb anomalies, café-au-lait spots, short stature, and pancytopenia with increased chromosomal breakage on DEB (or mitomycin C) testing define Fanconi anemia, a DNA-crosslink repair disorder (>75% FANCA/FANCC). Because FA cells are hypersensitive to DNA damage, transplant conditioning must be radiation-free with reduced doses of crosslinkers (e.g., cyclophosphamide). FA carries greatly increased risks of MDS/AML and squamous-cell solid tumors. DEB testing is specific for FA, not dyskeratosis congenita.

Q26
A 16-year-old boy has moderate pancytopenia with a hypocellular marrow.

Which statement is correct about this disorder?
  • AConcurrent androgens and G-CSF are recommended to maximize response
  • BThe mucocutaneous triad is present in Majoirty
  • CCorticosteroids are the treatment of choice
  • DPulmonary fibrosis is a complication of this disease
Correct answer: Pulmonary fibrosis is a complication of this disease

Short telomeres with a DKC1 mutation and the mucocutaneous features (nail dystrophy, reticulate pigmentation, oral leukoplakia) define a telomere biology disorder (classical dyskeratosis congenita). Androgens (oxymetholone or danazol) improve hematopoiesis in about two-thirds, and danazol can lengthen telomeres. Patients are at risk for pulmonary fibrosis (a common adult presentation) and cirrhosis. The classic triad is present in only a minority, and concurrent androgens + G-CSF is specifically discouraged (risk of splenic peliosis/rupture).

Q27
A 2-year-old girl has failure to thrive and isolated macrocytic anemia with reticulocytopenia. Marrow shows an erythroid maturation arrest (M:E >20:1) without dysplasia. An RPS19 mutation is identified; her mother has an abnormal thumb and childhood anemia.

Which finding supports this diagnosis?
  • AElevated red-cell adenosine deaminase (eADA) and elevated fetal hemoglobin
  • BIncreased chromosomal breakage with mitomycin C
  • CMarkedly short telomeres
  • DLow red-cell adenosine deaminase (eADA)
  • EExocrine pancreatic insufficiency
Correct answer: A — Elevated eADA and HbF support Diamond-Blackfan anemia.

A congenital pure red-cell aplasia presenting by age 2 with macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, erythroid arrest, an RPS19 (ribosomal protein) mutation, and a thumb anomaly is Diamond-Blackfan anemia, a ribosomopathy. Elevated red-cell adenosine deaminase (eADA) and elevated fetal hemoglobin help confirm the diagnosis. Increased MMC breakage indicates Fanconi anemia, short telomeres indicate a telomere disorder, and pancreatic insufficiency indicates Shwachman-Diamond syndrome.

Q28
A 61-year-old man with morbid obesity and a remote ventral hernia repair now has a large surgical bleed. He has no personal/family bleeding history. Preop PTT was 46.9 s (PT 12.7). Mixing study corrects and stays corrected. vWF:Ag 24%, FVIII:C 24%, vWF:RCo <12.5%. SPEP shows a monoclonal IgG kappa; DDAVP challenge gives minimal rise.

What is the diagnosis?
  • ACongenital type 3 von Willebrand disease
  • BLupus anticoagulant
  • CAcquired hemophilia A (factor VIII inhibitor)
  • DType 2N von Willebrand disease
  • EAcquired von Willebrand disease associated with MGUS
Correct answer: E — Acquired vWD in association with MGUS.

New-onset bleeding in older age with no lifelong/family history, low vWF antigen and activity (with disproportionately low RCo), a poor DDAVP response, and a monoclonal protein points to ACQUIRED von Willebrand disease, here associated with MGUS. The mixing study that corrects and stays corrected argues against a factor VIII inhibitor (acquired hemophilia A), in which the mix fails to correct (and worsens with incubation). Acquired vWD is linked to lymphoproliferative/plasma-cell disorders, MPN (especially ET), aortic stenosis (Heyde), and LVADs.

Q29
A man with severe hemophilia A and a -titer inhibitor (25 Bethesda units two weeks ago) on emicizumab arrives after a motor-vehicle accident with a headache.

What is the most appropriate immediate action?
  • AAdminister recombinant factor VIIa 90 µg/kg now
  • BGive recombinant FVIII 200 U/kg q12h now
  • COrder a head CT before deciding whether to give any factor
  • DGive intermediate-purity FVIII (Humate-P) 50 U/kg q12h now
  • EAdminister FEIBA now
Correct answer: A — Give a bypassing agent (rFVIIa) immediately; do not wait for the CT.

A high-responding inhibitor cannot be overcome with factor VIII, so a bypassing agent — recombinant factor VIIa (or activated PCC/FEIBA) — is required. In a known hemophiliac with head trauma, the appropriate product must be given immediately on arrival, before imaging or factor levels. Caution: if the patient were on emicizumab, FEIBA is avoided due to thrombotic risk, and rVIIa is used for breakthrough bleeds.

Q30
A young adult with lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding has a NORMAL platelet count and normal platelet size. this the platelet aggregation study
What is the diagnosis?
  • ABernard-Soulier syndrome
  • BStorage pool disease
  • CAspirin effect
  • Dvon Willebrand disease
  • EGlanzmann thrombasthenia
Correct answer: E — Glanzmann thrombasthenia: absent aggregation to all agonists but normal ristocetin.

Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a defect of platelet GPIIb/IIIa (the fibrinogen receptor), so aggregation is absent to all physiologic agonists (ADP, epinephrine, collagen, arachidonate) with no primary wave, while ristocetin-induced agglutination (which depends on GPIb–vWF, not GPIIb/IIIa) is normal; clot retraction is impaired and platelet count/size are typically normal. This is the mirror image of Bernard-Soulier, where ristocetin is the abnormal response.

Q31
A 19-year-old man with sickle cell disease experiences recurrent vaso-occlusive crises despite supportive care. He asks whether L-glutamine would help reduce his complications.

You explain that the recommendation is based on the landmark Phase III trial. (Niihara et al., NEJM 2018), Which of the following best summarizes the study findings?
  • AL-glutamine reduced vaso-occlusive crises, hospitalizations, and acute chest syndrome.
  • BL-glutamine increased hemoglobin by ≥1 g/dL in over 50% of patients.
  • CL-glutamine eliminated vaso-occlusive crises in more than 90% of patients.
  • DL-glutamine primarily reduced transfusion requirements without affecting pain crises.
Correct answer: L-glutamine reduced vaso-occlusive crises, hospitalizations, and acute chest syndrome.

Q32
A 67-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer developed a pulmonary embolism 8 months ago and has been receiving apixaban 5 mg twice daily. She asks whether she can lower the dose because she has experienced frequent bruising but still requires long-term anticoagulation.

You explain that the recommendation is based on the API-CAT trial which is comparing 5 mg bid vs 2.5 bid apixaban after 6 months. Which of the following best summarizes the study findings?
  • AReducing apixaban to 2.5 mg twice daily increased recurrent VTE but reduced major bleeding.
  • BReducing apixaban to 2.5 mg twice daily increased recurrent VTE no difference major bleeding
  • Cno difference in bleeding or VTE
  • DAll incorrect
Correct answer: All incorrect

Q33
A different patient has lifelong bleeding with THROMBOCYTOPENIA and GIANT platelets on smear.

Which pair of diagnosis and distinguishing test best fits?
  • AGlanzmann thrombasthenia; corrected by adding normal fibrinogen
  • BType 2B vWD; corrected by ristocetin at low dose
  • CBernard-Soulier syndrome (GPIb/IX/V defect); the absent ristocetin response is NOT corrected by adding normal plasma/vWF,
  • DStorage pool disease; absent dense granules on EM only
  • EAspirin effect; resolves in 7 days
Correct answer: C — Bernard-Soulier: macrothrombocytopenia, isolated absent ristocetin response not corrected by adding vWF.

Bernard-Soulier syndrome results from deficiency of the GPIb/IX/V complex (the vWF receptor), producing thrombocytopenia with giant platelets and an absent/markedly reduced ristocetin response, while ADP, epinephrine, and collagen responses are normal. The key discriminator from von Willebrand disease (which also has a reduced ristocetin response) is that in BSS the platelets themselves are defective, so adding normal plasma/vWF does NOT correct the ristocetin response, whereas in vWD it does.

Q34
An asymptomatic 25-year-old woman who has never had a VTE is referred because her brother has had a VTE and is HOMOZYGOUS for the prothrombin G20210A mutation. She is considering combined oral contraceptives and may want to become pregnant.

What is the most appropriate approach?
  • ADo not test — thrombophilia status never alters management in asymptomatic relatives
  • BTesting is reasonable here, because a positive result would lead you to advise against estrogen-containing contraception and to provide ante- and postpartum anticoagulation
  • CBegin indefinite anticoagulation now on the basis of the family history
  • DTest only male relatives, since women derive no benefit from knowing their status
  • EOrder factor VIII and homocysteine levels as the most useful screen
Correct answer: B — Testing is reasonable when a positive result changes contraceptive/pregnancy management.

The source supports considering thrombophilia testing of family members when a “strong thrombophilia” is present in the index patient AND the result will change management. In a woman contemplating estrogen contraception and pregnancy, a positive result is actionable: advise against estrogen-containing contraceptives and give ante-/postpartum prophylaxis. Testing is not a reflex (option A is too absolute), does not by itself justify treating an asymptomatic person with anticoagulation (C), and factor VIII/homocysteine are not recommended routine tools.

Q35
A 32-year-old woman has had three early pregnancy losses and no history of thrombosis. Previous testing showed she is heterozygous for factor V Leiden. She asks whether low-molecular-weight heparin during her next pregnancy will prevent miscarriage.
  • AStart prophylactic LMWH throughout the next pregnancy to prevent loss
  • BCombine low-dose aspirin with LMWH, as this prevents loss in inherited thrombophilia
  • CEvidence does NOT support routine LMWH to prevent pregnancy loss in wominherited thrombophilia,
  • DSwitch to a DOAC during pregnancy for convenience
  • EHer heterozygous factor V Leiden is the established cause of the losses and mandates anticoagulation
Correct answer: C — Advise against routine LMWH for pregnancy loss, and against routine thrombophilia testing in this setting.

Q36
A 22-year-old man with severe hemophilia A and high-titer factor VIII inhibitors has frequent spontaneous joint bleeds despite episodic treatment with bypassing agents. He asks whether the newly available emicizumab would provide better protection against bleeding.
You explain that the recommendation is based on the HAVEN 1 trial. Which of the following best summarizes the study findings?
  • AEmicizumab reduced bleeding compared with no prophylaxis but increased hospilization
  • BEmicizumab significantly reduced thrombosis and bleeding compared with bypassing agents.
  • CEmicizumab reduced bleeding and mortality
  • DEmicizumab reduced bleeding only
Correct answer: Emicizumab reduced bleeding only

Q37
A 21-year-old man with sickle cell disease and chronic hemolytic anemia asks:

"I read about a medication called voxelotor. Would I benefit from it?"

You explain that its use is based on the HOPE trial. Which of the following best summarizes the study findings?
  • AVoxelotor significantly increased hemoglobin levels but did not significantly reduce vaso-occlusive crises.
  • BVoxelotor reduced vaso-occlusive crises by approximately 50%.
  • CVoxelotor eliminated the need for transfusions.
  • DAll incorrect
Correct answer: Voxelotor significantly increased hemoglobin levels but did not significantly reduce vaso-occlusive crises.

Q38
A 50-year-old male presents with profound fatigue and petechiae. Workup confirms severe aplastic anemia (SAA): cellularity is 15%, ANC is $0.35 \times 10^9/\text{L}$, and platelets are $11 \times 10^9/\text{L}$. Cytogenetics are normal. He has no HLA-matched sibling donor and is scheduled to begin first-line immunosuppressive therapy (IST) with horse ATG and cyclosporine. He asks about adding eltrombopag to his regimen.
  • At significantly extends 2-year overall survival (OS).
  • BIt decreases the cumulative 5-year incidence of clonal evolution to MDS.
  • CIt significantly increases the 3-month complete response (CR) rate.
  • DB & C
Correct answer:

Q1
A 45-year-old man presents with dyspnea and petechiae. He is pale and tired. Labs: WBC 12, Hb 7, Plt 35, INR 1.2, PTT 24. Liver and kidney function are normal. LDH 1000. Smear shows abundant schistocytes, with high reticulocytes, high LDH, low haptoglobin, and a NEGATIVE DAT. ADAMTS13 activity returns at 45%. [IMAGE FROM SOURCE HERE] Which interpretation best fits this picture?
Q2
A 34-year-old woman is admitted with immune-mediated thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (iTTP). She is receiving plasma exchange, corticosteroids, and rituximab. On the second hospital day, she asks: "I read online about a new medication called caplacizumab. Would I benefit from it?" Which of the following is the most accurate response based on the HERCULES trial?
Q3

LDH is markedly elevated but haptoglobin is only borderline low and the reticulocyte count is LOW. Which is the most important next consideration before committing to plasma exchange?

Work-up shows the patient is IgA-deficient with a documented anti-IgA antibody, and no IgA-deficient donor unit is available. What is the most appropriate way to provide the next red-cell transfusion?
